fields.name: 12. Young people ‚Äì EU: which future?
fields.lectures: Understanding European Political and Economic Processes
fields.content-01:
1. Warm-up Activity: Silent Map Discussion (10 minutes)

‚Äç

  • Display a map of Europe (physical or digital)
  • Students silently mark:
    • Countries they have visited, studied in, or have personal connections with
  • Short guided discussion:
    • How do personal experiences influence our sense of being European?
    • Does mobility help people feel more connected to the EU?

‚Äç

2. Introduction to the Topic (10 minutes)

‚Äç

  • Introduce the central guiding question:
    How does the European Union invest in young people, and why does this matter for EU integration?
  • Briefly explain the lesson structure:
    • Group research
    • Poster creation
    • Short presentations

‚Äç

3. Group Research Phase (30 minutes)

‚Äç

Step 1: Group Division (5 minutes)

  • Divide students into 5 research groups
  • Each group receives one Appendix Research Card with a specific topic

Step 2: Research Task (25 minutes)

  • Groups research their topic using the guiding questions
  • Each group collects:
    • Key facts and definitions
    • Examples connected to young people
    • Why this topic is important for EU integration

‚Äç

4. Poster Creation (20 minutes)

‚Äç

  • Each group creates a poster or visual presentation (paper or digital)
  • Poster should include:
    • Title and key concepts
    • Short explanations (bullet points)
    • One concrete example or program
    • A clear link to young people and EU integration

‚Äç

5. Group Presentations (15 minutes)

‚Äç

  • Each group presents their poster (2‚Äì3 minutes per group)
  • Audience task:
    • Write down one new thing learned from each presentation

6. Reflection & Wrap-up (5 minutes)

  • Whole-class reflection:
    • Which topic felt most relevant to young people?
    • What surprised you about the EU?
    • How connected do you personally feel to the EU after this lesson?

‚Äç
fields.content-02:
Should the European Union Invest More in Young People to Strengthen EU Integration?

‚Äç

Lesson focus

‚Äç

The core aim of this lesson is for students to work in research groups, each exploring a different aspect of the European Union. Students research their assigned topic, create a visual poster, and present their findings to the class, focusing on how their topic connects to young people and European integration.

Objectives:

  • Explore how different EU institutions and policies contribute to European integration
  • Understand the role of young people in shaping the future of the EU
  • Develop research, critical thinking, and information synthesis skills
  • Practice structured argumentation and public speaking
  • Encourage active engagement with European policies and initiatives
fields.content-03:
Learning Outcomes

‚Äç

‚ÄçBy the end of the lesson, students will be able to:

  • Explain key aspects of the European Union and its institutions
  • Analyze how youth investment supports (or fails to support) EU integration
  • Construct and defend arguments using researched information
  • Recognize different perspectives on EU policies
  • Participate confidently in a debate setting
fields.content-04:
Materials and Tools

‚Äç

  • Internet access (phones, tablets, or computers)
  • Whiteboard / flipchart and markers
  • Timer
  • Debate format handouts (simplified WS format)
  • Appendix research cards (one per group)
  • Optional: Mentimeter, Jamboard, Padlet
fields.content-05:
‚ÄçAppendix: Research Task Cards

‚Äç

Research Task 1: History of the European Union
Explore how and why the European Union was created.

Guiding Questions:

  • When and why was the EU founded?
  • What problems was it meant to solve?
  • How has the EU changed over time?
  • Why is peace an important part of EU identity?
  • How can understanding EU history help young people feel more connected to it?

‚Äç

Research Task 2: European Parliament
Understand the role of the European Parliament.

Guiding Questions:

  • What is the European Parliament?
  • Who are Members of the European Parliament (MEPs)?
  • How does the Parliament represent citizens?
  • How can young people influence or participate in the Parliament?
  • Why is democratic representation important for EU integration?

‚Äç

Research Task 3: European Commission
Explore the role of the European Commission.

Guiding Questions:

  • What is the European Commission responsible for?
  • How does it propose and implement EU policies?
  • How does the Commission support youth-related programs?
  • Why is a strong executive body important for EU cooperation?

‚Äç

Research Task 4: EU Youth Programs and Opportunities
Research EU programs aimed at young people.

Guiding Questions:

  • What are Erasmus+ and the European Solidarity Corps?
  • Who can participate in these programs?
  • What skills do young people gain through them?
  • How do these programs strengthen European identity?
  • Should the EU invest more in such programs? Why or why not?

‚Äç

Research Task 5: Economic and Social Impact of Youth Investment

Analyze how investing in young people affects the EU‚Äôs future.

Guiding Questions:

  • How does youth unemployment affect EU stability?
  • What are the long-term benefits of investing in education and skills?
  • Are there risks or downsides to increased youth investment?
  • How does youth investment relate to economic growth and social cohesion?
  • How does this connect to EU integration?
